metaTitle: Cybercrime Laws Bangladesh 2026 – DSA, CSA, Penalties
title: Cybercrime Laws Bangladesh 2026: Digital Security Act, Cyber Security Act, and Your Rights
metaDescription: Complete 2026 guide to cybercrime laws in Bangladesh — Digital Security Act 2018, Cyber Security Act 2023, penalties, your legal rights, and how to defend yourself.
heroIntro: Bangladesh has undergone a significant transformation in its approach to cybercrime regulation over the past several years. The controversial Digital Security Act 2018 (DSA) was replaced by the Cyber Security Act 2023 (CSA), which itself has been subject to ongoing debate and proposed amendments in 2025–2026. Whether you are accused of a cyber offence, a journalist facing charges, a business dealing with data breaches, or a citizen concerned about online free speech, this comprehensive guide explains the cybercrime legal landscape in Bangladesh for 2026.

quickAnswer.heading: ⚡ Quick Answer: Cybercrime Laws Bangladesh 2026
points: The Cyber Security Act 2023 (CSA) replaced the Digital Security Act 2018 on 18 August 2023.
The CSA retains many of the same offences as the DSA but with slightly modified penalties.
Key offences include: hacking, spreading false information, pornography, defamation online, and disruption of critical infrastructure.
Penalties range from 1 year imprisonment up to 14 years depending on the offence.
Cases are filed before the Cyber Tribunal established under the CSA.

toc: The Cyber Security Act 2023: Key Provisions
Major Cybercrime Offences and Penalties in Bangladesh
Who Can File a Cybercrime Complaint?
The Cyber Tribunal: Jurisdiction and Procedure
Your Legal Rights If Accused Under the CSA

2026 Updates: Proposed Amendments and Reform Efforts

sections.content:
Bangladesh's journey to regulate digital crime has been contentious and evolving. The key milestones are:

  • ICT Act 2006 (Section 57): The Information and Communication Technology Act 2006 — particularly the notorious Section 57 — was Bangladesh's first major instrument for regulating online content. Section 57 criminalised publication of material deemed 'false, obscene, defamatory, or tending to deprave and corrupt.' It was widely criticised for its vague language and misuse against journalists and critics.
  • Digital Security Act 2018 (DSA): Enacted on 8 October 2018, the DSA replaced Section 57 but drew intense international criticism from press freedom organisations including Reporters Without Borders, the Committee to Protect Journalists, and the United Nations Human Rights Committee. Critics argued it was even more draconian than Section 57, with broader offences, police powers of search and seizure without warrant, and non-bailable provisions.
  • Cyber Security Act 2023 (CSA): Following sustained domestic and international pressure, the DSA was repealed and replaced by the Cyber Security Act 2023, which received presidential assent on 18 August 2023. While the CSA retains many of the same offences, some penalties were reduced and certain provisions were made bailable. However, civil society groups continue to raise concerns about the CSA's scope and potential for misuse.
  • 2025–2026 Reform Discussions: The interim government that came to power in August 2024 initiated consultations on further amendments to the CSA, with proposed changes aimed at decriminalising some online speech offences and narrowing the scope of actionable content.

sections.h2: The Cyber Security Act 2023: Key Provisions
sections.content:
The Cyber Security Act, 2023 (CSA) is the primary legislation governing cybercrime in Bangladesh as of 2026. Its key structural features are:

  • Scope: The CSA applies to all offences committed using digital devices, the internet, computer systems, or electronic communication within Bangladesh, and to Bangladeshi citizens committing such offences outside the country.
  • Cyber Tribunal: Cases under the CSA are tried before dedicated Cyber Tribunals established under the Act. The Dhaka Cyber Tribunal is the primary forum, with provisions for establishing tribunals in other divisions.
  • Police Powers: Police officers designated under the CSA have powers to search and seize digital devices, arrest suspects, and investigate cyber offences — in some cases without a prior warrant.
  • Cognisable and Non-Cognisable Offences: Some CSA offences are cognisable (police can arrest without a warrant) while others are non-cognisable (prior court permission needed). This distinction is critical for accused persons.
  • Bailable and Non-Bailable: Compared to the DSA, more offences under the CSA are bailable — meaning the accused can obtain bail from the police or a magistrate without approaching the Sessions Court or High Court. However, serious offences remain non-bailable.
  • Extraterritorial Application: The CSA applies to Bangladeshi nationals committing offences from abroad — reflecting the global nature of cybercrime.

If you or someone you know has been charged under the CSA, immediately consult a criminal lawyer in Bangladesh with specific cyber law experience.

sections.h2: Major Cybercrime Offences and Penalties in Bangladesh
sections.content:
The CSA defines a wide range of cybercrime offences. The most significant are:

  • Illegal access to computer/network (Hacking): Unauthorised access to any computer system, network, or database. Penalty: up to 7 years imprisonment and/or fine up to Tk. 25 lakh (first offence); up to 14 years and/or Tk. 1 crore (repeat offence).
  • Spreading false/offensive information online: Publishing material online that is false, offensive, threatening, or likely to disturb public order. This is one of the most widely used sections against journalists and critics. Penalty: up to 5 years and/or fine up to Tk. 10 lakh.
  • Online defamation: Publishing defamatory statements through digital means. Penalty: up to 3 years and/or fine up to Tk. 5 lakh.
  • Online pornography: Publishing, transmitting, or distributing obscene or pornographic material online. Penalty: up to 10 years for first offence; up to 14 years for repeat offence.
  • Identity theft and fraud: Using another person's identity through digital means for fraudulent purposes. Penalty: up to 5 years and/or fine.
  • Disruption of critical infrastructure: Attacking or disrupting computer systems used for critical national infrastructure (banking, power, telecommunications). Penalty: up to 14 years and/or fine up to Tk. 1 crore.
  • Cyberbullying / Online harassment: Harassing, intimidating, or threatening individuals through digital means — especially against women and children. Penalty: up to 3–5 years depending on the severity.
  • Spread of malware / ransomware: Deploying malicious software to damage systems or extort payments. Penalty: up to 7–14 years depending on circumstances.

sections.h2: Who Can File a Cybercrime Complaint?
sections.content:
Under the CSA, a cybercrime complaint can be initiated in two ways:

  1. Direct Police Complaint (for Cognisable Offences): For cognisable offences under the CSA, the victim or any aggrieved person can file a complaint directly with the police. The designated Cyber Police (under the Digital Security Agency or the CTTC - Counter Terrorism and Transnational Crime unit) can take immediate investigative steps.
  2. Tribunal Complaint (for Non-Cognisable Offences): For non-cognisable offences, the complainant must file a complaint directly before the Cyber Tribunal, which will decide whether to take cognisance and direct police to investigate.

Key points for complainants:

  • Preserve all digital evidence before filing a complaint — screenshots with timestamps, URL links, device logs, and witness contact details.
  • For social media harassment or defamation, document the offending posts before they are deleted by the author.
  • You may also file a complaint online through the Bangladesh Police's cybercrime portal.
  • Businesses suffering data breaches or ransomware attacks should also notify the Bangladesh Telecommunication Regulatory Commission (BTRC) and the Digital Security Agency.

If you have been harmed by a cybercrime and need legal assistance to pursue a case, consult a criminal lawyer in Dhaka who handles cyber law cases.

sections.h2: The Cyber Tribunal: Jurisdiction and Procedure
sections.content:
The Cyber Tribunal established under the CSA has exclusive jurisdiction over all offences under the Act. Key procedural features:

  • Location: The principal Cyber Tribunal is located in Dhaka, with the Sessions Judge (Cyber Tribunal) presiding. The government can establish additional tribunals in other divisions as needed.
  • Fast-Track Proceedings: The CSA mandates that Cyber Tribunal trials should be completed within 180 working days. In practice, complex cases take longer, but the tribunal is designed to be faster than regular civil and criminal courts.
  • Bail Applications: Bail applications for non-bailable CSA offences are heard by the Tribunal (or the Sessions Judge acting as Tribunal Judge). The High Court Division can also hear bail applications under its Section 498 CrPC jurisdiction.
  • Appeals: Appeals from Cyber Tribunal decisions go to the High Court Division of the Supreme Court of Bangladesh.
  • Evidence Standards: Digital evidence — screenshots, electronic records, server logs, device forensics — is admissible under the Evidence (Digital) provisions of the CSA read with the Evidence Act, 1872. Courts apply standards of authentication and reliability to digital evidence.

If you are facing trial before the Cyber Tribunal, engage an experienced criminal defence lawyer with cyber law expertise immediately. The specialised nature of these proceedings requires specialised advocacy.

sections.h2: Your Legal Rights If Accused Under the CSA
sections.content:
Being charged under the CSA is serious, but you have constitutional and statutory rights throughout the process:

  • Right to Legal Counsel: Under Article 33 of the Constitution and Section 340 CrPC, every accused person has the right to be defended by a lawyer of their choice. You must be informed of this right at the time of arrest.
  • Right Against Self-Incrimination: You cannot be compelled to testify against yourself (Article 35(4) of the Constitution).
  • Right to Know the Charges: You must be informed of the specific charges against you in a language you understand.
  • Right to Bail: For bailable CSA offences, you have a right to bail as a matter of law. For non-bailable offences, bail is discretionary — approach the Cyber Tribunal or the High Court Division under Section 498 CrPC.
  • Right to a Fair Trial: You have the right to cross-examine prosecution witnesses, present your own defence witnesses, and address the court through your lawyer.
  • Right to Appeal: If convicted, you have the right to appeal to the High Court Division and, with leave, to the Appellate Division of the Supreme Court.
  • Protection from Illegal Search: While police have broad powers under the CSA, searches must comply with constitutional protections. Any evidence obtained through an illegal search may be challenged in court.

sections.h2: Online Free Speech and the Limits of Cybercrime Law
sections.content:
One of the most contested areas of Bangladesh's cybercrime law is the relationship between criminal regulation of online content and the constitutional guarantee of freedom of expression under Article 39 of the Constitution.

The tension plays out in several key areas:

  • Political Criticism Online: Posting critical content about government policies or officials online has led to numerous arrests under the DSA and CSA. While the Constitution protects free speech, Article 39 allows restrictions in the interests of public order and morality — provisions that have been broadly interpreted by prosecution authorities.
  • Journalism and Investigative Reporting: Multiple journalists have been prosecuted under the DSA/CSA for investigative reporting. International organisations have consistently called these prosecutions attacks on press freedom.
  • Satire and Parody: Satirical content about public figures online has resulted in cybercrime complaints. Courts have generally held that bona fide satire is protected expression, but this defence requires careful legal argumentation.
  • Religious Sentiment: Content deemed offensive to religious beliefs can attract CSA charges as well as charges under the Penal Code. These cases often become politically charged.

If you are a journalist, blogger, or content creator facing a CSA complaint related to online expression, consulting a criminal lawyer with cyber law expertise is essential to mount a proper constitutional defence.

sections.h2: 2026 Updates: Proposed Amendments and Reform Efforts
sections.content:
As of 2026, the cybercrime legal landscape in Bangladesh remains in flux. Key developments include:

  • 2024 Post-Uprising Government: Following the political changes of August 2024, the interim government of Bangladesh established a law reform commission tasked with reviewing the CSA and proposing amendments that better balance security concerns with civil liberties.
  • Proposed Decriminalisation: Reform proposals include decriminalising certain speech offences currently under the CSA (e.g., online defamation becoming a civil rather than criminal matter), and requiring a court order before police can arrest in speech-related cyber cases.
  • Institutional Reform: There are proposals to establish an independent Digital Rights Commission to oversee the application of cybercrime laws and handle complaints about their misuse.
  • International Pressure: Bangladesh continues to face scrutiny from international bodies including the UN Human Rights Council regarding the use of cybercrime laws against journalists and activists.
  • BTRC and Digital Regulation: The Bangladesh Telecommunication Regulatory Commission (BTRC) has been given expanded powers to regulate digital content, raising new questions about the scope of digital speech regulation beyond the CSA.

This evolving landscape makes it essential to consult a current and up-to-date cybercrime lawyer in Bangladesh for the latest position on any specific legal issue you face.

faqs.question: Is sharing someone's private photos online a crime in Bangladesh?
faqs.answer: Yes. Non-consensual sharing of intimate or private images online — commonly called 'revenge porn' — is a criminal offence under the Cyber Security Act 2023, punishable by up to 5 years imprisonment and/or a fine. Victims can file a complaint with the Cyber Police or directly before the Cyber Tribunal.

faqs.question: Can I be arrested for a comment I made on Facebook in Bangladesh?
faqs.answer: Yes, under certain circumstances. If the comment is deemed to be false, offensive, defamatory, or to disturb public order, it can attract a cybercrime case under the CSA. The applicable section determines whether the offence is bailable or non-bailable, and thus whether police can arrest without a warrant.

faqs.question: What should I do immediately if I am arrested under the Cyber Security Act?
faqs.answer: Immediately request a lawyer. Do not make any statement to police without your lawyer present. Contact your family or a trusted person to arrange legal representation. If the offence is bailable, apply for bail at the police station or before the Magistrate. If non-bailable, your lawyer must apply before the Cyber Tribunal or the High Court Division.

faqs.question: What is the difference between the DSA 2018 and the CSA 2023?
faqs.answer: The Cyber Security Act 2023 replaced the Digital Security Act 2018. Key differences include slightly reduced penalties for some offences, reclassification of some offences from non-bailable to bailable, and minor changes to the definitions of offences. However, civil society critics argue the CSA retains the fundamental flaws of the DSA and continues to threaten free speech.

faqs.question: Can a company or business be prosecuted for cybercrime in Bangladesh?
faqs.answer: Yes. The CSA provides for corporate liability — companies and their officers can be prosecuted for cybercrime offences committed through the company's systems or by its employees acting in the course of their employment. Businesses should implement robust cybersecurity policies and consult a cyber law lawyer to ensure compliance.
